question: Do you maintain a current inventory of all hardware assets (servers, workstations, mobile devices)?
fix_hint: Start with a simple spreadsheet listing every device, its owner, OS version, and whether it stores sensitive data.

smb_context.Healthcare: Include any device storing ePHI. Required under HIPAA Security Rule §164.310(d).
smb_context.Finance: Include devices processing payment or account data. Required for PCI-DSS scope definition.

question: Do you maintain a current inventory of all software assets and licenses?
fix_hint: List all installed applications and SaaS services. Free tools like Belarc Advisor can auto-discover installed software.

question: Are unauthorized devices automatically detected and prevented from accessing your network?
fix_hint: For small offices, start with MAC address filtering on your router/AP. For larger networks, consider a NAC solution.

smb_context.Healthcare: For small practices, this could mean setting up a separate guest Wi-Fi and restricting the clinical network to known devices.
smb_context.Finance: Segment payment processing devices on their own VLAN.

question: Do you have a formal process for decommissioning and securely wiping retired assets?
fix_hint: Write a one-page media disposal policy. Use DBAN (free) or a certified e-waste vendor with a Certificate of Destruction.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires documented media sanitization. Never sell, donate, or discard devices without a verified wipe.

question: Are all assets assigned a designated owner responsible for their security?
fix_hint: Add an 'Owner' column to your asset inventory. For small teams, the office manager or IT contact is a reasonable default.

question: Do you enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all user accounts?
fix_hint: Enable Security Defaults in Microsoft 365 (free) or turn on 2FA in Google Workspace. Takes 30 minutes, prevents 99.9% of account compromise.

smb_context.Healthcare: MFA is considered an addressable standard under HIPAA. Opting out requires documented justification.
smb_context.Finance: PCI-DSS 4.0 requires MFA for all access to cardholder data environments.

question: Do you enforce the principle of least privilege for all user access rights?
fix_hint: Review who has admin access and downgrade standard users. Most employees need Standard User, not Administrator.

smb_context.Healthcare: Front desk staff should not have the same Dentrix/EHR permissions as clinicians.

question: Are user access reviews conducted at least quarterly?
fix_hint: Set a quarterly calendar reminder. Pull your M365/Google Workspace user list and verify every account is still active and appropriate.

question: Do you have a formal process for revoking access upon employee termination or role change?
fix_hint: Create a one-page offboarding checklist: disable email, change shared passwords, revoke VPN/remote access, collect devices. Takes 15 minutes to write.

smb_context.Healthcare: Former employees with active EHR access is a leading cause of HIPAA breach investigations.

question: Do you enforce a strong password policy (minimum length, complexity, expiration)?
fix_hint: NIST now recommends 12+ character passphrases without forced rotation. Configure this in your M365 or Google admin console.

question: Are privileged accounts (admin, root) managed with a dedicated privileged access management solution?
fix_hint: For small teams, start by using separate admin accounts (not your daily login) and storing admin passwords in a password manager like Bitwarden (free).

smb_context.Healthcare: For small practices, this means not logging into Windows as Administrator for daily work. Use a standard account and elevate when needed.
smb_context.Finance: PCI-DSS requires unique IDs for each person with computer access — no shared admin accounts.

question: Do you perform regular vulnerability scans on all networked systems?
fix_hint: OpenVAS (free) or Nessus Essentials (free for 16 IPs) can run quarterly scans. For very small offices, even Windows Defender's periodic scan counts as a start.

question: Are critical and high-severity patches applied within 14 days of release?
fix_hint: Enable automatic Windows Updates on all machines. For the server, schedule a monthly maintenance window. Any end-of-life OS (Windows 7, Server 2012) must be replaced or isolated immediately.

smb_context.Healthcare: Unpatched systems storing ePHI are a top OCR enforcement target. EOL operating systems are indefensible in a breach investigation.

question: Do you have an automated patch management system for operating systems and applications?
fix_hint: Action1 (free for up to 200 endpoints) or PDQ Deploy can automate patching for small networks.

question: Do you conduct annual penetration testing by a qualified third party?
fix_hint: For small businesses, an annual external vulnerability scan ($500–2,000) is a reasonable alternative to a full pentest until budget allows.

smb_context.Finance: PCI-DSS requires quarterly ASV scans and annual penetration testing for merchants processing card payments.

question: Do you track and remediate identified vulnerabilities using a formal ticketing or tracking system?
fix_hint: Even a shared spreadsheet tracking 'vulnerability, severity, owner, due date, status' satisfies this control for small teams.

question: Is sensitive data encrypted at rest and in transit?
fix_hint: Enable BitLocker on all Windows Pro laptops (free, built-in). Enforce HTTPS/TLS on your website and email. FileVault on Macs.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA treats encryption as an addressable safeguard. If you don't encrypt, you must document why and what alternative protects ePHI. In practice, just encrypt.
smb_context.Finance: PCI-DSS requires encryption of cardholder data at rest and in transit. No exceptions.

question: Do you perform regular backups of critical data and systems?
fix_hint: Follow the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media types, 1 offsite. Cloud backup services with HIPAA BAAs start at ~$30/month (Carbonite, Acronis).

smb_context.Healthcare: A USB drive next to the server is not a backup strategy — ransomware encrypts attached drives. You need an offsite/cloud copy.

question: Are backups tested regularly to verify successful restoration?
fix_hint: Test a restore quarterly. Pick one critical file or database, restore it to a test location, and verify it opens correctly. Document the date and result.

question: Do you have a formal data classification policy that categorizes data by sensitivity?
fix_hint: A simple three-tier model works: Public, Internal, Confidential. Map your data types to these tiers in a one-page document.

question: Are data loss prevention (DLP) controls in place to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration?
fix_hint: For small teams, start by disabling USB mass storage via Group Policy and restricting personal email forwarding in M365/Google Workspace.

smb_context.Healthcare: For small practices, this means ensuring staff can't email patient lists to personal accounts or copy them to personal USB drives.

question: Do you have a documented incident response plan?
fix_hint: Download HHS OCR's free IRP template or NIST SP 800-61. Customize it in an afternoon: add your IT contact, a HIPAA attorney, your cyber insurance hotline, and the HHS breach reporting URL.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires a breach notification plan. You have 60 days from discovery to notify HHS and affected individuals for breaches of 500+ records.

question: Is the incident response plan tested at least annually through tabletop exercises or simulations?
fix_hint: A tabletop exercise can be a 30-minute team meeting: 'Imagine we got ransomware today — what do we do first?' Walk through your IRP together.

question: Are roles and responsibilities clearly defined for incident response team members?
fix_hint: For small teams: designate one person as the incident lead, one as the communications lead. Add their names and phone numbers to the IRP.

question: Do you have centralized log collection and monitoring (SIEM or equivalent)?
fix_hint: For small businesses, enabling audit logging in M365/Google Workspace and reviewing the sign-in logs monthly is a practical starting point. Full SIEM solutions like Wazuh are free but require technical setup.

smb_context.Healthcare: For small practices, this could mean enabling Dentrix audit logging and reviewing it monthly, plus checking M365 sign-in reports.

question: Do you have established communication protocols for notifying stakeholders during a security incident?
fix_hint: Add a 'Communications' section to your IRP with: who notifies patients, who contacts the insurance carrier, who handles media inquiries, and template breach notification letters.

question: Do you conduct security assessments of third-party vendors before onboarding?
fix_hint: Create a simple vendor security questionnaire (5–10 questions). Ask: Do you encrypt data? Do you have a SOC 2? Will you sign our BAA? Many vendors provide this proactively.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires due diligence on any vendor that will access, store, or transmit ePHI.

question: Do vendor contracts include security requirements and data protection clauses?
fix_hint: For each vendor that touches sensitive data, confirm a signed BAA or DPA exists. Most major vendors (Microsoft, Google, Intuit) have standard templates available on request.

smb_context.Healthcare: A signed Business Associate Agreement is legally required for every vendor handling ePHI. No exceptions, no verbal agreements.

question: Do you maintain an inventory of all third-party services that have access to your data?
fix_hint: Build a one-page vendor register: vendor name, what data they access, BAA status, contract renewal date. Most small businesses undercount their vendors by 50%.

question: Do you periodically reassess the security posture of existing vendors?
fix_hint: Review your vendor register annually. Check if vendors have had breaches, confirm BAAs are current, and verify you still need each vendor's access.

question: Have you identified all regulatory frameworks applicable to your organization (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOX, GDPR, etc.)?
fix_hint: Common triggers: accept credit cards → PCI-DSS. Handle health info → HIPAA. Have EU customers → GDPR. Publicly traded → SOX. Most small businesses have at least PCI-DSS exposure.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA applies to all covered entities and their business associates. If you bill insurance or handle PHI, you are covered. PCI-DSS also likely applies if you process credit card payments.
smb_context.Finance: PCI-DSS, SOX (if publicly traded), GLBA, and state privacy laws likely apply.

question: Do you have documented information security policies reviewed and updated at least annually?
fix_hint: SANS provides free policy templates at sans.org/information-security-policy. Customize a set of 5–10 core policies for your organization.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires written policies and procedures for the Security Rule. Lack of documentation is the #1 OCR audit finding.

question: Do you conduct regular internal or external compliance audits?
fix_hint: For small businesses, an annual self-assessment using this tool counts as an internal audit. Document the results and remediation actions taken.

question: Do you maintain evidence of compliance activities (audit logs, policy acknowledgments, training records)?
fix_hint: Create a 'Compliance Evidence' folder. Save training completion certificates, signed policy acknowledgments, and assessment reports. Regulators want to see documentation, not just effort.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires 6-year document retention. Keep training records, risk assessments, and policy versions.

question: Are server rooms and network infrastructure physically secured with access controls?
fix_hint: Install a keypad lock on the server closet ($50–100). Set up a basic webcam (Wyze, $30). Keep the door locked during and after business hours.

question: Are visitor access logs maintained and reviewed?
fix_hint: A paper sign-in sheet at the front desk satisfies this control. Log name, company, purpose, and time in/out.

question: Do you have environmental controls (fire suppression, climate control, UPS) for critical infrastructure?
fix_hint: An APC UPS ($150–250) prevents power-related database corruption and gives you 10–15 minutes to shut down cleanly during outages.

question: Do all employees receive security awareness training upon hire and at least annually thereafter?
fix_hint: KnowBe4, Compliancy Group, and HIPAA Training offer affordable plans ($10–30/user/year). Enroll all staff, track completion dates.

smb_context.Healthcare: HIPAA requires workforce security training. Document completion dates — OCR will ask for them.

question: Do you conduct regular phishing simulation exercises?
fix_hint: KnowBe4 offers free phishing simulation tools. Run one campaign per quarter. It's the single best way to measure whether training is working.

question: Are employees trained on identifying and reporting social engineering attacks?
fix_hint: Include social engineering scenarios in your annual training. Cover phone pretexting, USB drops, and tailgating — not just email phishing.

question: Do you provide role-specific security training for IT staff and administrators?
fix_hint: For small teams without dedicated IT, ensure whoever manages your systems completes at least one cybersecurity fundamentals course annually (Cybrary and CISA offer free courses).
